perturbation.original_text: (2) You and your representatives agree that the Confidential Evaluation Material will be used solely for the purpose of evaluating the proposed transaction. You also agree that you and your officers, employees, financing sources, consultants, Affiliates, agents and advisors, herein collectively referred to as “ your representatives ,” will not disclose or permit the disclosure of any of the Confidential Evaluation Material now or hereafter received or obtained from the Company or its representatives to any third party or otherwise use or permit the use of the Confidential Evaluation Material in any way detrimental to the Company, except as required by court order or legal process, without the prior written consent of the Company, provided, however, that any such information may be disclosed to such of your representatives who need to know such information for the purpose of evaluating the proposed transaction and who are advised of this agreement and agree to keep such information confidential and to be bound by this agreement to the same extent as if they were parties hereto, it being understood that you shall be responsible for any breach of this agreement by your representatives; provided, however, that you shall not be responsible for any failure to comply with the terms of this agreement by any of your representatives that (i) is not one of your officers, employees or Affiliates and (ii) has signed an undertaking in favor of the Company and you to the effect of Annex A hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: (2) You and your representatives agree that the Confidential Evaluation Material will be used generally for the purpose of evaluating the proposed transaction. You also agree that you and your officers, employees, financing sources, consultants, Affiliates, agents and advisors, herein collectively referred to as “ your representatives ,” will not disclose or permit the disclosure of any of the Confidential Evaluation Material now or hereafter received or obtained from the Company or its representatives to any third party or otherwise use or permit the use of the Confidential Evaluation Material in any way detrimental to the Company, except as required by court order or legal process, without the prior written consent of the Company, provided, however, that any such information may be disclosed to such of your representatives who need to know such information for what they consider to be the purpose of evaluating the proposed transaction and who are advised of this agreement and agree to keep such information confidential and to be bound by this agreement to the same extent as if they were parties hereto, it being understood that you shall be responsible for any breach of this agreement by your representatives; provided, however, that you shall not be responsible for any failure to comply with the terms of this agreement by any of your representatives that (i) is not one of your officers, employees or Affiliates and (ii) has signed an undertaking in favor of the Company and you to the effect of Annex A hereto.
perturbation.explanation: The terms 'generally' and 'what they consider to be the purpose' introduce ambiguity into the restrictive use of confidential information. This undermines the legal obligation to use the material solely for the specified purpose, which can lead to potential misuse or disclosure without clear accountability.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Trade Secrets Act states that there should be no misappropriation of trade secrets, and that a trade secret is information that is kept confidential. By using phrases such as 'generally' and 'what they consider to be the purpose,' this change would likely be a violation of the California Trade Secret Act if the information would then be considered a trade secret and there was misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'generally' and 'what they consider to be the purpose,' which weakens the original restrictive language. This undermines the obligation to use the material solely for the specified purpose. The law snippet defines 'Misappropriation' as 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' By making the purpose of use more subjective, the changed text increases the risk of misappropriation because it broadens the scope of permissible use, thereby contradicting the intent to maintain secrecy and potentially violating the California Trade Secrets Act as explained by the LLM, which states there should be no misappropriation of trade secrets, and that a trade secret is information that is kept confidential.

perturbation.original_text: (3) In the event you or your representatives receive a request to disclose Confidential Evaluation Material pursuant to any court order or legal process, you or your representatives will give the Company prompt written notice thereof so that the Company may seek an appropriate protective order. You and your representatives agree to cooperate (at the Company’s expense) as reasonably requested by the Company in its efforts to maintain the confidentiality of such Confidential Evaluation Material. If you or your representatives are ultimately required to disclose such Confidential Evaluation Material, you or your representatives shall disclose only so much thereof as necessary to comply with such court order or legal process.
perturbation.changed_text: (3) In the event you or your representatives receive a request to disclose Confidential Evaluation Material pursuant to any court order or legal process, you or your representatives will endeavor to give the Company written notice thereof so that the Company may attempt to seek a protective order. You and your representatives agree to cooperate as they feel is appropriate (at the Company’s expense) in the Company's efforts to maintain the confidentiality of such Confidential Evaluation Material. If you or your representatives are ultimately required to disclose such Confidential Evaluation Material, you or your representatives shall disclose what they deem is reasonably necessary to comply with such court order or legal process.
perturbation.explanation: The phrases 'endeavor to give,' 'as they feel is appropriate,' and 'what they deem is reasonably necessary' introduce ambiguity and discretion regarding the duty to notify and cooperate in maintaining confidentiality. This weakens the obligation to protect confidential information under court orders or legal processes, potentially violating discovery rules.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 2017.010 defines the scope of discovery, including the ability to obtain information that is not privileged, that is relevant to the subject matter of the pending action. The original agreement requires cooperation by you and your representatives. The modified agreement uses words such as 'endeavor' and 'as they feel is appropriate' and 'what they deem is reasonably necessary' which creates loopholes that could be used to avoid the intention of the original agreement to cooperate and, in doing so, may be violating this California code.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 2017.010 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 2. Scope of Discovery [2017.010 - 2017.320] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) ARTICLE 1. General Provisions [2017.010 - 2017.020] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2017.010. Unless otherwise limited by order of the court in accordance with this title, any party may obtain discovery regarding any matter, not privileged, that is relevant to the subject matter involved in the pending action or to the determination of any motion made in that action, if the matter either is itself admissible in evidence or appears reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. Discovery may relate to the claim or defense of the party seeking discovery or of any other party to the action. Discovery may be obtained of the identity and location of persons having knowledge of any discoverable matter, as well as of the existence, description, nature, custody, condition, and location of any document, electronically stored information, tangible thing, or land or other property. (Amended by Stats. 2012, Ch
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguities that weaken the obligation to protect confidential information under court orders or legal processes, potentially violating discovery rules. The original agreement required cooperation. The modified agreement uses terms such as 'endeavor,' 'as they feel is appropriate,' and 'what they deem is reasonably necessary,' creating loopholes. According to California Code of Civil Procedure § 2017.010, 'any party may obtain discovery regarding any matter, not privileged, that is relevant to the subject matter involved in the pending action...'. The changes could be used to avoid the intention of the original agreement to cooperate, thus violating the California code. The original text created a clear obligation, while the changed text opens the door for the party to decide how much to disclose or cooperate.

perturbation.original_text: (6) At any time upon the Company’s request, all Confidential Evaluation Material (and all copies, summaries, and notes of the contents or parts thereof) shall be returned or, if you so choose, destroyed and not retained by you or your representatives in any form or for any reason and written certification to that effect will be sent by you to the Company within 30 days of such request; provided, however, that you and your representatives shall not be obligated to return or destroy the Confidential Evaluation Material if, and to the extent, otherwise required by any applicable law, regulation, policy or procedures relating to the retention, back-up storage or automatic archiving of files and data. Any Confidential Evaluation Material that is retained by you or your representatives for the sole purpose of compliance with such law, regulation, policy or procedures shall not be used for any purpose other than to evidence compliance therewith and such retained material and information shall continue to be subject to the confidentiality obligations set forth in this letter agreement, which obligations shall survive any termination hereof. All the Confidential Evaluation Material will be and remains the property of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: (6) At any time upon the Company’s request, all Confidential Evaluation Material (and all copies, summaries, and notes of the contents or parts thereof) should, if possible, be returned or, if you so choose, destroyed and not retained by you or your representatives in any form or for any reason, and a certification to that effect should be sent by you to the Company within a reasonable time of such request; provided, however, that you and your representatives shall not be obligated to return or destroy the Confidential Evaluation Material if, and to the extent, otherwise required by any applicable law, regulation, policy or procedures relating to the retention, back-up storage or automatic archiving of files and data. Any Confidential Evaluation Material that is retained by you or your representatives for purposes they believe are compliant with such law, regulation, policy or procedures shall not be used for any purpose other than to evidence compliance therewith and such retained material and information shall continue to be subject to the confidentiality obligations set forth in this letter agreement, which obligations shall survive any termination hereof. All the Confidential Evaluation Material will be and remains the property of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be returned' to 'should, if possible, be returned,' and 'within 30 days' to 'within a reasonable time,' introduces ambiguity. Additionally, changing 'for the sole purpose of compliance' to 'for purposes they believe are compliant' creates a loophole based on subjective belief. This weakens the return obligation, conflicting with the intent to protect confidential information and comply with California law regarding the return of property.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Penal Code § 484 defines theft as unlawfully taking the property of another. By weakening the obligation to return the Confidential Evaluation Material and creating a subjective standard for retaining it, the modified text increases the risk that the material might not be returned as requested, potentially leading to a scenario that could be construed as theft, especially if the retention is based on a misinterpretation of compliance needs.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, PEN 484 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Penal Code - PEN PART 1. OF CRIMES AND PUNISHMENTS [25 - 680.4] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 13. OF CRIMES AGAINST PROPERTY [450 - 593g] ( Title 13 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Larceny [484 - 502.9] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 484. (a) Every person who shall feloniously steal, take, carry, lead, or drive away the personal property of another, or who shall fraudulently appropriate property which has been entrusted to him or her, or who shall knowingly and designedly, by any false or fraudulent representation or pretense, defraud any other person of money, labor or real or personal property, or who causes or procures others to report falsely of his or her wealth or mercantile character and by thus imposing upon any person, obtains credit and thereby fraudulently gets or obtains possession of money, or property or obtains the labor or service of another, is guilty of theft. In determining the value of the property obtained, for the purposes of this section, the reasonable and fair market value shall be the test, and in determining the value of services received the contract price shall be the test. If there be no contract price, the reasonable and going wage for the service rendered shall govern. For the purposes of this section, any false or fraudulent representation or pretense
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and subjectivity where the original text was more definitive, specifically weakening the obligation to return confidential material. The original contract states the material "shall be returned" within "30 days." The modified version changes this to "should, if possible, be returned" within "a reasonable time," creating ambiguity. Also, changing 'for the sole purpose of compliance' to 'for purposes they believe are compliant' introduces a subjective standard for retaining the confidential material. According to the Law Explanation, this change increases the risk that the material might not be returned, potentially leading to a scenario that could be construed as theft under California Penal Code § 484 if the retention is based on a misinterpretation of compliance needs. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referring to California Penal Code § 484, defines theft as including when someone "shall feloniously steal, take, carry, lead, or drive away the personal property of another, or who shall fraudulently appropriate property which has been entrusted to him or her." By making the return of the material less certain, the modified contract increases the risk of the material being unlawfully retained and thus, potentially 'stolen' as defined by the law. The change directly contradicts the intent to ensure the property of the company is returned.

perturbation.original_text: (10) You agree that for a period of one year from the date of this letter agreement, neither you nor your representatives will, without the prior written consent of the Company, directly or indirectly: (A) acquire, offer to acquire, or agree to acquire, directly or indirectly, by purchase or otherwise, any securities or direct or indirect rights to acquire any securities of the Company or any subsidiary thereof, or of any successor to or person in control of the Company, or any assets of the Company or any division thereof or of any such successor or controlling person; provided, however, that nothing herein shall prohibit the purchase of securities in the ordinary course of any of your representatives’ business that does not in any event result in aggregate ownership by such representative of more than 2% of the outstanding amount of any class of securities of the Company; (B) seek or propose to influence or control the management or policies of the Company, make or in any way participate, directly or indirectly, in any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as such terms are used in the rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) to vote any voting securities of the Company or any subsidiary thereof, or seek to advise or influence any person or entity with respect to the voting of any voting securities of the Company or any subsidiary thereof; (C) make any public announcement with respect to, or submit a proposal for or offer of (with or without conditions), any merger, recapitalization, reorganization, business combination or other extraordinary transaction involving the Company or any subsidiary thereof or any of their securities or assets; or (D) enter into any discussions, negotiations, arrangements or understandings with any third party with respect to any of the foregoing, or otherwise form, join or in any way engage in discussions relating to the formation of, or participate in, a “group” within the meaning of Section 13(d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, in connection with any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: (10) You agree that for a period of one year from the date of this letter agreement, neither you nor your representatives will, absent strong evidence to the contrary, directly or indirectly: (A) acquire, offer to acquire, or agree to acquire, directly or indirectly, by purchase or otherwise, any securities or direct or indirect rights to acquire any securities of the Company or any subsidiary thereof, or of any successor to or person in control of the Company, or any assets of the Company or any division thereof or of any such successor or controlling person; provided, however, that nothing herein shall prohibit the purchase of securities in the ordinary course of any of your representatives’ business that does not in any event result in aggregate ownership by such representative of more than 2% of the outstanding amount of any class of securities of the Company; (B) seek or propose to influence or control the management or policies of the Company, make or in any way participate, directly or indirectly, in any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as such terms are used in the rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) to vote any voting securities of the Company or any subsidiary thereof, or seek to advise or influence any person or entity with respect to the voting of any voting securities of the Company or any subsidiary thereof; (C) make any public announcement with respect to, or submit a proposal for or offer of (with or without conditions), any merger, recapitalization, reorganization, business combination or other extraordinary transaction involving the Company or any subsidiary thereof or any of their securities or assets; or (D) enter into some discussions, negotiations, arrangements or understandings with any third party with respect to any of the foregoing, or otherwise form, join or in some way engage in discussions relating to the formation of, or participate in, a “group” within the meaning of Section 13(d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, in connection with any of the foregoing.
perturbation.explanation: The phrases 'absent strong evidence to the contrary' and 'some discussions', and 'in some way' introduces ambiguity into the restrictions on acquiring securities and engaging in certain activities. What constitutes 'strong evidence' is not defined, and 'some discussions' is vague. This weakens the non-compete obligations and potentially conflicts with insider trading laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of any manipulative or deceptive device or contrivance in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. The modified language creates an environment where the definition of insider trading, and intent thereof, is open to interpretation and could weaken an action for an intent to manipulate data.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The altered text introduces ambiguity by using phrases like 'absent strong evidence to the contrary,' 'some discussions,' and 'in some way.' This ambiguity weakens the original non-compete obligations. The Law Explanation connects this to potential violations of Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, which prohibits manipulative or deceptive devices in securities trading. The altered language's ambiguity regarding intent could make it harder to prove a violation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referring to 15 USC 78j, reinforces the prohibition of manipulative devices. The introduction of ambiguous conditions weakens the enforceability of restrictions intended to prevent such manipulation, thus creating a contradiction.
